title: Accessible queries and interactions

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Accessible queries and interactions; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Testing Library; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Accessible queries and interactions
sources.claim: React tests should query by accessible role, name, label, and visible text in the same priority users perceive.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Accessible queries and interactions
sources.claim: React tests should query by accessible role, name, label, and visible text in the same priority users perceive.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React tests should query by accessible role, name, label, and visible text in the same priority users perceive.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A save button is selected by role and accessible name rather than its CSS class. Your goal is to write component tests that avoid implementation-detail selectors. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Accessibility, errors and performance; Routing and application context; Semantic and keyboard-accessible components; javascript quality dom tests. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React tests should query by accessible role, name, label, and visible text in the same priority users perceive. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Some hidden or presentation nodes lack roles, duplicate names need scoping, and test ids are a last resort. This draft therefore rejects: data-testid is always the most stable and user-centered query.

includes: Query rendered output by role, name, label or text and drive realistic user interactions.
Write component tests that avoid implementation-detail selectors.
excludes: Some hidden or presentation nodes lack roles, duplicate names need scoping, and test ids are a last resort.

outcomes: Write component tests that avoid implementation-detail selectors.

examples.title: Accessible queries and interactions: concrete case
examples.situation: A save button is selected by role and accessible name rather than its CSS class.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A save button is selected by role and accessible name rather than its CSS class.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Prefer semantic user-facing queries and use test ids only when no meaningful accessible selector exists.
examples.result: The result follows because Prefer semantic user-facing queries and use test ids only when no meaningful accessible selector exists.

examples.title: Accessible queries and interactions: boundary case
examples.situation: Some hidden or presentation nodes lack roles, duplicate names need scoping, and test ids are a last resort.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Some hidden or presentation nodes lack roles, duplicate names need scoping, and test ids are a last resort.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: data-testid is always the most stable and user-centered query.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Prefer semantic user-facing queries and use test ids only when no meaningful accessible selector exists.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: data-testid is always the most stable and user-centered query.
misconceptions.correction: Prefer semantic user-facing queries and use test ids only when no meaningful accessible selector exists.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: data-testid is always the most stable and user-centered query.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Accessible queries and interactions, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Accessible queries and interactions before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to write component tests that avoid implementation-detail selectors. Keep this boundary in view: Some hidden or presentation nodes lack roles, duplicate names need scoping, and test ids are a last resort.
